title: Cisco ISE Process Crash and Service-Restart Detection
dataSources: `index=nac sourcetype=cisco:ise:syslog` from the Splunk Add-on for Cisco Identity Services 5.0 ([Splunkbase 1915](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/1915)). Process state is reported under the *Internal Operations Diagnostics* and *System Statistics* logging categories. Key message codes: `60030` (process restart), `60031` (process restart failed), `60040` (process unresponsive — watchdog kill), `60050` (memory threshold exceeded for a service), `60060` (database connection lost), `52030` (admin-initiated service stop), `52031` (admin-initiated service start). ISE process names referenced in the `ProcessName` field: `application-server` (Tomcat — admin UI + REST), `database` (Oracle replicator), `runtime-AAA-Server` (RADIUS/TACACS+ engine), `MnT-MIB-Daemon`, `pxGrid-Server`, `ise-elasticsearch`, `redis-server`, `crl-server`, `licensemgr`, `replication-jvm`. CIM mapping: `Change.action=stopped|started|modified Change.object_category=service`.
app: `Splunk Add-on for Cisco Identity Services` ([Splunkbase 1915](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/1915)) on Heavy Forwarders + Search Heads.
spl:
index=nac sourcetype="cisco:ise:syslog" MessageCode IN (60030, 60031, 60040, 60060, 52030, 52031)
| rex "NodeName=(?<node>[^,\s]+)"
| rex "ProcessName=(?<process>[^,\s]+)"
| rex "AdminUser=(?<admin_user>[^,\s]+)"
| eval event_class = case(
    MessageCode=="60030", "unplanned_restart",
    MessageCode=="60031", "restart_failed",
    MessageCode=="60040", "watchdog_kill",
    MessageCode=="60060", "db_connection_lost",
    MessageCode=="52030", "admin_stop",
    MessageCode=="52031", "admin_start")
| stats count latest(_time) as last_event values(event_class) as classes by node, process
| sort - last_event
| where mvfind(classes, "unplanned_restart")>=0 OR mvfind(classes, "restart_failed")>=0 OR mvfind(classes, "watchdog_kill")>=0 OR mvfind(classes, "db_connection_lost")>=0
description: Detects Cisco ISE process crashes, watchdog-triggered service kills, and database connection losses on any deployment node. Differentiates planned admin-initiated service stops/starts from unplanned crashes so the alert only fires for genuinely unexpected service-state changes.
value: An unplanned restart of `runtime-AAA-Server` on a PSN means RADIUS authentications fail closed for 30–120 seconds. An unplanned restart of `pxGrid-Server` severs the SOC's context feed silently — Splunk ES and Cisco Secure Workload may keep running with a stale endpoint context for minutes before the gap is detected via the missing-events check. A `database` process restart on the PAN delays admin operations and can corrupt an in-flight bulk import. This UC catches all of these immediately and routes to the on-call ISE engineer.
implementation: ISE syslog is already collected via UC-17.1.28's data plumbing. This UC adds an additional saved search and uses the same dataset. Schedule every 5 minutes, search window `-15m@m to @m`, trigger on count > 0, throttle suppression on `node`+`process` for 30 min so a restart loop produces one page rather than dozens.
visualization: (1) Single value: "Unplanned restarts last 24h" (red >0). (2) Table: node | process | event_class | last_event | count. (3) Timeline (Splunk Timeline visualisation): events as marker points, colour by event_class, Y-axis = node. Reveals 'cluster of crashes at 14:32' patterns. (4) Sankey: process → event_class → node, last 7d. Useful for trending which processes are most fragile in the deployment.
dataModelAcceleration: Yes — these are `Change` events (`Change.action=stopped|started|modified Change.object_category=service`). If you accelerate the `Change` data model for 30 days, the trend panels become near-instant.

references.title: Splunk Add-on for Cisco Identity Services (Splunkbase 1915)
Cisco ISE Syslog Reference — Internal Operations Diagnostics
Cisco ISE 3.4 Admin Guide — Service Management

knownFalsePositives: (a) **Patch installation** — `application install` triggers planned restart of multiple processes. Correlate against the *Administrative and Operational Audit* category for the matching admin operation; suppress within 30 min of a 51020 (patch operation) event. (b) **Manual `application restart`** — generates 52030 + 52031 events; the search already filters these out (only fires on 60030/60031/60040/60060). (c) **JVM full-GC pause >60s** — the watchdog kills `application-server` mistaking the GC pause for a hang. This is rare but happens on under-provisioned PANs. Escalate to ISE platform team for heap tuning rather than to RADIUS on-call. (d) **`database` connection-lost during PAN failover** — when the Primary PAN fails over to Secondary, every other node logs 60060 once for the connection loss. Suppress 60060 events for 5 min after a 60030 on the Primary PAN. (e) **First-boot of a new node** — multiple processes start in sequence and emit 52031 events; this is expected and the search filters these.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Art.21(2)(b) requires incident handling. Service-crash detection is a primary input — uncontrolled restarts of access-control services may need to be reported as significant incidents under Art.23.
compliance.controlObjective: Provide timely detection of service-availability incidents in the access-control system to support NIS2 incident-handling and reporting obligations.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-17.1.30' alert log retained 7y; cross-referenced with the Art.23 24h/72h/1m incident-reporting timeline when a service crash escalates to a significant incident.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Art.17 (ICT-related incident management process) requires processes to detect, manage, and notify ICT incidents. Service-crash detection on a critical access-control system is exactly such detection.
compliance.controlObjective: Detect ICT-related incidents in the access-control plane to support DORA's incident-management process.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-17.1.30' alert log + linked DORA major-incident classification matrix when applicable.

compliance.assurance_rationale: AU-12 (Audit Record Generation) requires the system generate audit records for events including service-state changes. Forwarding ISE process events to Splunk evidences fulfilment.
compliance.controlObjective: Generate audit records for service-state changes in the access-control system.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-17.1.30' archived events + retention policy in audit_evidence index.

compliance.assurance_rationale: A.5.25 (Assessment and decision on information security events) requires the organisation assess and decide on the categorisation of security events. Process-crash detection feeds directly into the assessment process for the access-control plane.
compliance.controlObjective: Generate categorisable security events for assessment and decision-making.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-17.1.30' + linkage to the security-event-assessment runbook.

controlTest.positiveScenario: On a lab ISE PSN, SSH in and run `application stop ise` (this is admin-initiated and emits 52030, which the search excludes). Then SSH back in and forcibly kill the AAA process (e.g. `kill -9` of the runtime-AAA-Server PID). The watchdog will emit MessageCode 60040 and ISE will auto-restart the process emitting 60030 within 30s. UC-17.1.30 must fire showing that node + process with `event_class=watchdog_kill` and `unplanned_restart`.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Run a planned `application restart ise` on the same node. Only 52030 + 52031 events are emitted. UC-17.1.30 must NOT fire — the search's `MessageCode IN (60030, 60031, 60040, 60060, 52030, 52031)` deliberately includes 52030/52031 in the base filter so they are *visible to humans* in ad-hoc searches, but the final `where` clause excludes them via `mvfind(classes, "unplanned_restart")>=0 ...`.

detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Same as UC-17.1.28: ISE syslog forwarded to Splunk via the *Distributed Management*, *System Statistics*, *Internal Operations Diagnostics*, and *Administrative and Operational Audit* logging categories at WARN severity (52030/52031 require *Administrative and Operational Audit* at INFO).
• Splunk_TA_cisco-ise 5.0+ on Heavy Forwarders + Search Heads.
• `ise_deployment.csv` lookup with `node`, `persona`. Used to enrich the alert page with persona context.
• Splunk role with `srchIndexesAllowed = nac`.

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Verify the categories are forwarded:
```
Administration > System > Logging > Logging Categories
  Internal Operations Diagnostics: severity = WARN, target = Splunk-HF-1
  System Statistics: severity = WARN, target = Splunk-HF-1
  Administrative and Operational Audit: severity = INFO, target = Splunk-HF-1
```
And validate event flow:
```spl
index=nac sourcetype="cisco:ise:syslog" MessageCode IN (60030, 60031, 60040, 60060, 52030, 52031) earliest=-7d
| stats count by MessageCode, ProcessName, NodeName
```
A stable deployment over 7d should show single-digit counts for 52030/52031 (planned admin operations) and zero for 60030/60040/60060 — if you see 60030 events week after week, the platform has a chronic instability worth investigating.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Primary search:

```spl
index=nac sourcetype="cisco:ise:syslog" MessageCode IN (60030, 60031, 60040, 60060, 52030, 52031)
| rex "NodeName=(?<node>[^,\s]+)"
| rex "ProcessName=(?<process>[^,\s]+)"
| rex "AdminUser=(?<admin_user>[^,\s]+)"
| eval event_class = case(
    MessageCode=="60030", "unplanned_restart",
    MessageCode=="60031", "restart_failed",
    MessageCode=="60040", "watchdog_kill",
    MessageCode=="60060", "db_connection_lost",
    MessageCode=="52030", "admin_stop",
    MessageCode=="52031", "admin_start")
| stats count latest(_time) as last_event values(event_class) as classes values(admin_user) as admin_users by node, process
| sort - last_event
| where mvfind(classes, "unplanned_restart")>=0 OR mvfind(classes, "restart_failed")>=0 OR mvfind(classes, "watchdog_kill")>=0 OR mvfind(classes, "db_connection_lost")>=0
| lookup ise_deployment.csv node OUTPUT persona, site
```

Why `mvfind(...)>=0` instead of `=`?  `=` works for single-value fields but `classes` is a multi-value field after `values()`. `mvfind` returns the position of a match (>=0 if present, -1 if not).

Schedule: cron `*/5 * * * *`, time range `-15m@m to @m`, trigger on `count > 0`, throttle suppression on `node`+`process` for 30 min.

Step 3 — Validate
(a) Lab provoke. Pick a non-production PSN. SSH in:
```
ise/admin# show process status
ise/admin# # find the runtime-AAA-Server PID
ise/admin# tech sudo kill -9 <PID>
```
(`tech sudo` is the documented escape to run a privileged shell command in ISE.) Watch the syslog stream — within 5–15 seconds you should see MessageCode 60040 (watchdog kill) followed by 60030 (unplanned restart). Confirm the alert fires.

(b) Negative test. On the same node, `application restart ise`. Confirm the alert does NOT fire — only 52030/52031 events are emitted, and the where-clause filters them.

(c) Field validation:
```spl
index=nac sourcetype="cisco:ise:syslog" earliest=-30d MessageCode IN (60030, 60040)
| stats count by ProcessName
```
A stable deployment may show zero counts. If a single process (commonly `application-server` or `runtime-AAA-Server`) shows >5 events in a month, escalate to ISE platform team for root-cause.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Dashboard "ISE — Service Crash History":
• Single value: "Crashes last 24h" (red >0), "Crashes last 7d" (orange).
• Timeline panel: 30 days of events, colour by `event_class`, Y-axis = node.
• Top-N table: which (node, process) tuple has the most unplanned restarts last 30d.
• Sankey: process → event_class → site (from lookup) — useful when one site reports more crashes than others (DC environmental issue?).

Alerting:
• PagerDuty: high-urgency on first unplanned restart of `runtime-AAA-Server` or `database`. Medium-urgency on first unplanned restart of `application-server` or `pxGrid-Server`. Low on others.
• Auto-create SOAR ticket with: node + process + event_class + 'tech support bundle' job suggestion.

Runbook (owner: ISE on-call):
1. Identify node + process from the alert.
2. SSH to node: `show application status ise`. Confirm the process is currently running. If not, `application start ise` and re-check.
3. Capture a tech-support bundle while the issue is fresh: `tech support` from CLI. The bundle includes the just-crashed process's core file (if it crashed cleanly) and the last 60s of `cmm.log` / `localStore.log`.
4. Open Cisco TAC case if the crash recurs within 24h on the same process.
5. If the process is `runtime-AAA-Server` and the crash recurred: the deployment may be RADIUS-overloaded. Consider redistributing PSN load (NAD RADIUS server-list reordering) until the platform team has reviewed.

Step 5 — Troubleshooting

• **Alert fires repeatedly on `application-server` for the same node** — Tomcat memory leak. Check JVM heap settings: `tech support` bundle > tomcat.log. Long-term fix is heap tuning by Cisco TAC; short-term mitigation is a daily scheduled `application restart ise` during the maintenance window.

• **`runtime-AAA-Server` crashes during peak auth load** — PSN is undersized or has a misconfigured upstream identity store with poor latency. Cross-reference UC-17.1.40 (RADIUS latency) — if latency was >2s before the crash, the AAA process queue overflowed.

• **`db_connection_lost` (60060) on every node simultaneously** — The Primary PAN restarted or its database service flapped. Look for 60030 on the Primary PAN at the same timestamp. If present, root-cause the PAN crash first; the 60060 events from other nodes are the symptom, not the cause.

• **No 52030/52031 events appear when admins use `application restart`** — *Administrative and Operational Audit* category is not enabled at INFO. Switch it on; events should appear within 60s of the next admin action.

• **`AdminUser` field is null** — The TA's regex extracts only on the *Administrative and Operational Audit* category but the event was emitted under *Internal Operations Diagnostics* (no admin user attached). This is correct behaviour — for auto-generated events, the field is null. Do not depend on `admin_user` for non-52xxx codes.
grandmaExplanation: If a part of the access-control system stops working unexpectedly, like a service that quietly crashed and restarted itself, we ring the bell so a human investigates. We carefully ignore planned restarts and only alert when something stopped on its own.
